ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for geoduck clam stir-fried with XO sauce. FOODON_03301116 (clam food product) is the closest parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJW (Clams and cockles) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for geoduck clam prepared with XO sauce.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept identified for this specific prepared shellfish dish.

allergens: fish (XO sauce contains dried scallop and dried shrimp)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of geoduck clam stir-fried with XO sauce is approximately 250 g, usually shared among 2–4 diners as part of a Zi Char meal.

nutrient_source.title: Clam, cooked, moist heat (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy) combined with XO sauce component estimation from USDA Branded and FAO/INFOODS SEA reference values for stir-fried shellfish dishes
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for geoduck clam stir-fried with XO sauce exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or USDA FoodData Central. Values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for cooked clam (FDC ID 175159) as the primary protein/fat/mineral base, with adjustments for XO sauce contribution (added fat ~4 g/100 g, sodium ~300 mg/100 g, sugar ~1 g/100 g) based on USDA Branded XO sauce reference entries and FAO/INFOODS SEA stir-fried shellfish analogues. Energy recalculated using Atwater factors.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — shellfish and protein-dominant seafood dishes
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for geoduck clam stir-fried with XO sauce. GI estimated at 25 based on analogous protein-dominant shellfish dishes (cooked clam, cooked scallop) from the University of Sydney GI Database, which consistently show GI values in the 20–30 range due to negligible starch content. XO sauce contributes minor carbohydrate from dried seafood and chilli; overall dish carbohydrate is low (~5 g/100 g), supporting a low GI classification. GL per 250 g serving = (25 × 5.2 × 2.5) / 100 = 3.25, rounded to 3.3.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from XO sauce and soy sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sauce preparation, as a 250 g serving provides approximately 1300 mg sodium, representing over 50% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and low carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 25 and only 5.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g, this dish has a low glycaemic impact and is generally suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in moderate portions as part of a balanced meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate fat from XO sauce, offset by beneficial omega-3 fatty acids from geoduck clam
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content warrants caution for individuals with cardiovascular disease; however, geoduck clam provides lean protein and trace omega-3 fatty acids, and saturated fat is relatively low at 1.4 g/100 g, so occasional moderate consumption is acceptable within a heart-healthy dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Geoduck Clam Stir-Fried with XO Sauce (象拔蚌XO酱炒) is a premium Zi Char and Malaysian-Chinese seafood dish featuring geoduck clam (Panopea generosa), a large, long-necked saltwater clam prized for its firm, crunchy siphon and sweet, briny flavour. The clam is sliced thinly and wok-fried at high heat with XO sauce — a luxury Cantonese condiment made from dried scallop, dried shrimp, chilli, garlic, and oil — along with aromatics such as ginger, garlic, and spring onion. The result is a dish with a complex umami depth, mild heat, and a satisfying chewy-crisp texture. Geoduck is particularly valued in Chinese culinary culture for its texture and perceived nutritional richness. Nutritionally, the dish is high in protein (~14.5 g/100 g), moderate in fat (~7.8 g/100 g), and low in carbohydrate (~5.2 g/100 g), with an exceptionally high vitamin B12 content (~22 mcg/100 g) and notable iron (~13.5 mg/100 g). The primary clinical concern is sodium, which is elevated due to XO sauce and soy sauce components (~520 mg/100 g). The glycaemic index is low (~25), making it suitable for blood sugar management. It is not halal due to the presence of dried shrimp and pork-derived ingredients sometimes found in XO sauce. This dish is commonly served in Singapore and Malaysian seafood restaurants and Zi Char stalls as a shared centrepiece dish.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for geoduck clam stir-fried with XO sauce was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy cooked clam data with XO sauce component adjustments from USDA Branded and FAO/INFOODS SEA references. GI is analogously estimated from protein-dominant shellfish dishes in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the estimation methodology is systematic and the base ingredient (cooked clam) has strong USDA SR Legacy support.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated composites; no single approved-source entry covers this exact dish. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy clam entry or FAO/INFOODS SEA shellfish references. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source data for cooked clam or XO sauce components. 2. Trans fat is confirmed 0.0 per USDA SR Legacy cooked clam data and typical XO sauce formulations. 3. The food_group has been corrected from the input value 'Oils, seasonings and condiments' to 'Fish and seafood', as geoduck clam is the primary ingredient and the dish is a seafood preparation. 4. The food_name has been corrected from 'Gooduck Clam' (apparent OCR/transcription error) to 'Geoduck Clam'. 5. Halal status is false because traditional XO sauce contains dried shrimp and may contain pork-derived ingredients. 6. GI of 25 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database shellfish references; no direct measurement exists for this dish. 7. Sodium content is a significant clinical concern; the XO sauce component contributes the majority of sodium.
